data.title: Baird Equity Opportunity Fund Q1 2026 Commentary And Market Outlook
data.description:
Fellow shareholders,

Equities struggled for traction during the first quarter, challenged by the war in Iran, concerns over the direction of monetary policy, and, to a lesser extent, uncertainty over how the uptake of Artificial Intelligence (AI) could negatively impact prominent elements of the broader economy. The Russell 2000 Index posted a 0.89% total return for the quarter. The Baird Equity Opportunity Fund underperformed, generating a net return of -11.39%. *

Following a solid start to the year, equities sold off toward the latter part of the quarter due to the conflict in the Middle East, though the Russell 2000 managed to eke out a 0.89% gain. While our 3-month results did not follow, we are encouraged by the relatively solid performance of small-caps. From a sector standpoint, energy, materials, and industrials led the way. By contrast, consumer, healthcare, and technology – software, in particular – posted notable declines. Sector moves during Q1 were a headwind to portfolio results given our smaller exposure to energy and materials and larger stakes in software and related shares.

Market weakness and sector impacts aside, the war in Iran caused few direct impacts on the portfolio. While a spike in the oil price impacted Norwegian Cruise Line’s stock price (NCLH, down 16% in the quarter), it helped boost shares of energy holding Oceaneering International (OII, up 48% in the quarter). Indirectly, however, the war has disrupted the formerly expected path of accelerating economic growth and more accommodative monetary policy, sapping some enthusiasm for small-cap stocks in March. Unlikely just three months ago, it is now a proverbial coin flip whether the next Fed action is an interest rate cut or a hike thanks to elevated energy prices and disrupted supply chains brought on by the conflict.

More impactful to our Q1 performance was the heightened exuberance for all things related to AI. The consensus narrative is that, to reap the benefits of the power of AI, the buildout of necessary infrastructure will last as far as the eye can see. At the same time, a powerful overhang has emerged that AI will render most software companies “damaged goods” or even existentially obsolete as AI agents proliferate and replicate their work for a fraction of the cost. Look no further than the staggering disparity between the performance of semiconductor stocks, broadly perceived to be “AI winners”, and software stocks: in the first quarter alone, the iShares Semiconductor ETF outperformed the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF by over 33%.

Despite our best efforts in planning for this possibility, including elimination of software names Gitlab (GTLB) and Blackbaud (BLKB), and the fortuitous acquisition of Confluent (CFLT) by IBM (IBM), the portfolio nonetheless experienced secondary effects that impacted Q1 results. BILL Holdings (BILL) and NCR Voyix (VYX) represented collateral damage, both selling off with the broader software group despite being much less exposed to the AI-driven fears that have gripped the sector. Both names were among our top three detractors in the first quarter.

In BILL’s case, its business model is led by payments, with BILL receiving a percentage of each dollar that it processes in the payment stream between small businesses. This represents the majority of the company’s revenue and would be hard to replace with an agent. However, while software subscriptions generate only a fifth of the company’s revenue, it hasn’t stopped BILL from finding itself as a perceived AI loser, presumed guilty until proven innocent. Even strong results in February and a rumored go-private transaction were only enough to help the stock for a short period of time before revisiting its recent lows.

Similarly, VYX was pummeled despite building evidence that the one-year-old turnaround is entering a more prosperous phase. With a well-regarded new product set, nascent customer wins, a fortified management team and balance sheet, and building business momentum, the stock felt poised to rise from its bargain-basement valuation at the start of 2026. Instead, it was unable to escape the clutches of negative software sentiment, and the stock finished the quarter down 38%. Trading with a roughly 20% free cash flow yield, management is now stepping up its share buyback, which could eliminate over a quarter of the outstanding shares upon completion.

All told, our direct and indirect software holdings explain almost three-quarters of the portfolio's underperformance in Q1 despite representing less than one-quarter of our invested capital. Regardless of what we say or how we try to explain it, owning practically anything remotely related to software has proven to be costly thus far in 2026. Moving forward, while we maintain conviction in key positions like NCR Voyix and BILL Holdings, we are not pressing the issue by proverbially "doubling down" given that the sector is currently viewed by many investors as uninvestible due to the threat of AI.

Our top winners during the period were Mister Car Wash (MCW), Oceaneering International (OII), Madison Square Garden Sports (MSGS), and RBC Bearings Incorporated (RBC). After several years of languishing in the public markets, Mister Car Wash advanced when it received a take-private bid in February. Oceaneering benefited from good results – and, of course, being in the "right neighborhood" of energy. Madison Square Garden Sports rose on hopes that the company might spin off its holdings in the New York Knicks, possibly a preliminary step to selling a valuable stake in the franchise which could help close the gap between the stock's current market value and the value of its collection of professional sports franchises. Industrial name RBC Bearings continues to report strong fundamentals, while investor appetite for the sector has pushed its valuation higher.

The past twelve or so months have proven challenging for the portfolio. Our investment team and process remain unchanged. What has changed is the combination of 2025's tariff escalation, 2026's war in Iran, and the light speed advances in AI, which together have ratcheted up the difficulty for traditional bottom-up investors like us. Tariffs and war abruptly elevated economic uncertainty, leading to less overall macro-optimism and diminished excitement (e.g. valuation) for our holdings despite solid fundamental performance. At the same time, the AI infrastructure buildout has offered an escape valve of sorts to investors seeking confidence in a singular growth pillar. For those of us not willing to chase exuberant AI-related valuations, it has proved to be quite the perfect storm.

It may be that the environment remains constant - dominated by news of tariffs and war and whatever else might come, the markets obsessed with a narrow set of companies that trade on price momentum at high valuations with no contemplated fundamental hiccups along the way. It may turn out that the fundamentally sound, cash-generating companies in which we have invested simply see their valuations forever impaired until their terminal value is fully erased.

While plausible, this is not the scenario plan under which we are investing. It seems too easy. Often, such a path is the wrong one to take. In the late 1990's, internet stocks were easy to own. In the mid-2000's, China-leveraged commodity stocks were all the rage. In the early part of this decade – ironically – software-as-a-service (SaaS) names rocketed to new heights. The internet and China are indeed powerful today, but the journey has been volatile to reach this destination. It remains to be seen what the market holds for SaaS stocks now that AI tumult has disrupted its journey. And now AI itself has ascended to the mantle of being revered.

Our belief is that its journey may prove to be more circuitous than currently implied. Not because AI isn't amazing technology; but because the lessons of history tell us to be careful. Recent surveys show there is much consternation beneath the surface of AI's promise, including the fear over lost jobs, apprehension over rising electricity costs due to rising data center demand, and the ever-present "not in my backyard" chorus from those that do not want AI data centers near their homes. In fact, a March Quinnipiac poll found that 80% of those surveyed are concerned about AI, and over half believe AI will do more harm than good in their day-to-day lives. Additionally, AI's current insatiable thirst for components could soon disrupt entire industries' ability to get necessary parts, such as automobiles or consumer products, while sharply rising global energy prices and increasing supply chain constraints create new debottlenecking challenges. None of this will likely affect AI's destination – but it could erect speed bumps along the journey and create unforeseen volatility in AI-related shares and themes.

In 1962, President Kennedy gave a moving speech at Rice University to highlight the importance of investing in the US Space program. His quote that "we do not do these things because they are easy, but because they are hard" has always resonated with me. Over the past thirteen years, our team has often bemoaned taking the hard path of making outsized returns in small-cap idiosyncratic ideas. And while our recent results may call that very strategy into question, our long-term track record emboldens us to not choose the quick fix of chasing momentum at any price. Instead, conviction in our team, process, long-term horizon and most importantly, our portfolio, are unwavering. It might be the harder path forward, but we believe it to be the correct one.

We look forward to sharing another update in three months.

Respectfully,

Joe Milano

data.title: TFLO: Cash Is King In A Fragile Geopolitical Moment
data.description:
The iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO) is effectively a cash bet, that is to say virtually zero duration, though offering a floating rate yield on that investment and with no corporate credit angle whatsoever. It is about the same expense ratio as (FLOT), also iShares but including corporate bond exposures as well at 0.15%. We'd maybe be inclined to take a little more risk at these short durations than just Treasuries, since the FLOT is indexed towards banking, where we're not too concerned about issues at the moment in the current environment. But broadly speaking, we'd favour staying away from duration at the moment or from equities - in other words, cash is king. The ceasefire is looking more fragile by the day, and the inflationary pressures are remounting as oil rises once again. As opposed to a more optimistic August a little bit ago, the futures curve is now pointing to a normalisation in November.

Macro Discussion

We think the current market environment favours cash bets tactically such as TFLO - that is to say cash over longer duration yield alternatives, and cash instead of equities.

Cash Bull Case

  • Equities continue to climb to new highs despite serious threats to tech-led equity markets, at least when talking about the US. The concern with equities is that while the AI-trade continues to drive markets, multiples for the (SPY) continue to climb as higher multiple stocks gain in the value-share mix. European PE ratios, while also somewhat higher than long-term averages, pale at 17x compared to the 27x PE ratio of the US market, significantly above its longer term average of 20x. What's important is that the higher the multiple of the market, the higher the expectations of future earnings growth. Therefore, more of the valuation is loaded into the market implied DCF's horizon values, which are most sensitive to cost of capital concerns. As of writing, oil is once again above $105 per barrel by the Brent. The yield curve, representative of the bond market view (often considered the smart money) is close to its recently high levels. Reinflation risks are being taken seriously by the bond market with ceasefire concerns reflecting in oil prices back to highs again yet equity markets continue to drive forward, principally indexed to tech bets (notice the higher climb of the (XT) compared to the broader market). We think this divergence poses a significant risk to equities in their increasingly narrow rally, with performance so concentrated in AI bets also at the expense of companies whose business models appear to be more at risk.

  • Much the same argument holds for cash over duration bets. Longer duration is going to be standing in the way of reinflation concerns, which are becoming more apparent after diplomatic efforts seem to be getting dialed back with trip cancellations and trade through Hormuz remains limited. The ceasefire is looking fragile as no real diplomatic headway is seeming to be made, although at least on Thursday the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire was extended.

Cash Bear Case

  • As opposed to cash bets, duration may make some sense in the fixed income space on account of headlines such as the probe into Powell being dropped, which was one of the effective conditions for Powell leaving the position and for Warsh to be able to come in, who is Trump's dove. However, we say right away that given the energy crunch situation, we doubt that the Fed even with a new chair would decide to cut rates in this exact moment. The issue of inflation is simply too concerning amid a significant rise in oil and therefore gas prices, which has a big effect on the American consumer and expectations of inflation.
  • As for the bull case for equities, while technical effects threaten horizon values through cost of capital considerations, and these really matter for tech valuations, it is also the case that key semiconductor players are seeing excess demand, including Intel (INTC) and that the demand case for AI is still very real. It could be an opportunity missed.

Bottom Line

We'd maybe inclined towards the slightly more aggressive FLOT which has a YTM of 4.25% versus the 3.8% of the TFLO. On one hand, a smaller proportion goes to that 0.15% expense ratio, and on the other there is the fact that we aren't particularly worried about the financial system in this moment, and are happy with the banking skew and supranational financial institutions as well in corporate credit exposures. We don't think it's needed to play it quite as safe as Treasuries.

However, we do think that cash bets such as TFLO, FLOT and others that are on the effectively zero duration side of the yield curve, inure an investor from possible duration risks, and perhaps even more so from the risks of an equity market that is rising despite risk factors related to the Iran War, whose cost of capital effects on markets really matter for tech, aren't abating. Taking profit and benefiting from cash's optionality in a market panic may be the play over the tactical short term while the potential outcome of peace talks, which aren't really properly occurring, are unclear.

data.title: Federal Signal's Fantastic Growth Still Doesn't Justify Upside
data.description:
One company that has seen a surge in its share price over the last year has been Federal Signal Corporation (FSS). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ back in April of 2025, the stock has risen 65.5%. That's meaningfully above the 41.1% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. Back then, I remained cautious about the valuation of the company and the uncertainty that the company was facing. But since then, management has done an exceptional job to expand the top and bottom lines of the business. Moving forward, we should expect further improvement. But that doesn't necessarily mean that investors would be wise to buy in. The way I see it, the stock looks pretty much fairly valued on an absolute basis and it looks pricey compared to most other similar firms. Although backlog is growing and that should fuel additional expansion in the long run, as things stand, I cannot in good faith rate it any higher than the ‘hold’ I called it back then.

It is important to be flexible when it comes to analyzing companies. When the data changes to justify a change in opinion, you should be willing to update how you stand on a business accordingly. Soon, investors will have data covering one additional operating quarter for this prospect. And as we approach that date, analysts actually seem quite bullish. In fact, they expect revenue, profits, and adjusted profits, to rise year over year. So this should definitely be top of mind for shareholders in the coming days.

The picture hasn’t changed

Operationally speaking, I find Federal Signal Corporation to be an interesting business. The firm has a long history, dating back to 1901. From those humble origins back then, it has grown into a large enterprise with a market capitalization of $7.14 billion. Today, it focuses on providing a wide array of products and integrated solutions for customers in the municipal and governmental, as well as industrial and commercial spaces. Specifically, it produces vehicles and equipment that are used for maintenance and infrastructure end markets like sewer cleaners, industrial vacuum loaders, excavation trucks, street sweepers, water blasting equipment, and more. It also is responsible for producing public safety equipment like vehicle light bars and sirens, public warning systems, an industrial signaling equipment.

The firm has achieved some really strong growth over the last year. For context, back in 2024, revenue was $1.86 billion. Today, that number stands at $2.18 billion. That's a 17.1% jump over the course of a year. As revenue for the company improved, profitability improved also. Net income jumped from $216.3 million to $246.6 million. On an adjusted basis, the rise was from $206.3 million to $260.2 million. Operating cash flow, meanwhile, rose from $231.3 million to $254.7 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a jump from $301 million to $362.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $350.6 million to $438.9 million.

This is a pretty consistent picture. But to best understand what is driving this upside, we should look at each of its operating segments. Both of them have increased over the last year. But the real driver of its expansion is its Environmental Solutions Group, which focuses on everything other than its public safety equipment. Year over year, revenue skyrocketed 18% from $1.56 billion to $1.84 billion. The company benefited here from a surge in the number of orders from $1.54 billion to $1.86 billion. It appears as though this overall increase was thanks to higher demand for refuse trucks amounting to $147.4 million. However, almost all of this was the result of additional backlog that the company acquired when it purchased New Way, a producer of refuse collection vehicles that the company acquired in November of 2025 in exchange for $424.2 million. This is not to say that's the business did not see other growth here. For instance, aftermarket offerings grew $51.6 million year over year. Higher demand for safe digging trucks boosted sales by $46.8 million. Plus, road-marking and line removal equipment revenue increased $43.8 million.

As revenue for the segment increased, profitability improved also. Segment profits expanded from $261.2 million to $324.6 million. Greater revenue certainly helped here. However, management also mentioned that higher prices played a role in the increase in revenue. To the extent that those exceeded inflationary pressures, there is the potential for profit margin expansion. In fact, the profit margin for the segment went from 16.8% to 17.7% on a year over year basis. Clearly, the company is doing a fine job in this regard.

Meanwhile, we have its Safety and Security Systems segment. Year over year, revenue surged from $304.4 million to $343 million. Management chalked this up to a rise in orders from $306.2 million to $363.7 million. It appears as though a lot of this was thanks to higher domestic government spending. However, the firm did also see an 18% rise in revenue associated with customers from outside of the US. This increase in revenue brought with it higher profits also. Segment profits expanded from $64.4 million up to $81.5 million, pushing the profit margin for the unit up from 21.2% to 23.8%.

Overall, I would say that things are going pretty well for the company. And in all likelihood, we can expect this trend to continue in the near term. In the chart above, you can see backlog and orders for the company on a segment-by-segment basis. Both of these have hit new highs in 2025. Again, a lot of this can be attributed to acquisition activities. After all, management allocated $501 million in cash payments to the purchase of other enterprises. That dwarfed the $39.7 million spent the previous year and the $55 million spent the year before that. But growth is growth all the same and it should be appreciated, especially when profits and cash flows follow suit.

What is particularly promising is that management expects revenue for this year to come in at between $2.55 billion and $2.65 billion. At the midpoint, that would be 14.7% above what the company saw for last year. Management believes that adjusted earnings per share from this should come in between $4.50 and $4.80. Hitting the midpoint of those expectations would mean adjusted net income of $283.1 million compared to the $260.2 million that the business reported for 2025. If we scale up other profitability metrics accordingly, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of about $394.8 million and EBITDA totaling $477.5 million.

Armed with these figures, and the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced right now. Honestly, these aren't bad multiples. But they certainly don't put the company in the undervalued range. Crucially, compared to other similar firms, the stock is still quite pricey. On both a price to operating cash flow basis and an EV to EBITDA basis, four of the five firms that I compared it to in that chart ended up being cheaper than it is. And on a price to earnings basis, it is the cheapest of the group.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned that management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year. That is expected to occur before the market opens on April 29th. The good news is that analysts believe that revenue will rise from $463.8 million last year to $579.4 million this year. As the table below illustrates, both earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share our forecasted increase also. The table also shows certain cash flow metrics. It is highly probable that these cash flow figures will increase as well. I would especially be interested in what adjusted operating cash flow ends up being.

Takeaway

The way I see things right now, Federal Signal Corporation remains a fascinating business. But there's a big differentiator between fascinating and attractive. Management has certainly been successful in supercharging revenue growth by means of acquisition activities over the last year or so. Having said that, the stock is anything but cheap. The market has priced in this expansion, and has even pushed the share price up higher than most other similar firms. In light of this, the best thing that I can do is to rate it a ‘hold’ right now.

data.title: Deckers: As Hoka Turns The Corner, So Can The Stock
data.description:
Deckers (DECK) has been on a wild ride the last few years. From being a darling consumer discretionary stock to one of the worst performers in retail, the stock has been trading sideways YTD and investors are looking to the upcoming report for direction. As HOKA compares finally start to get easier in the quarters ahead, the Q4 earnings report late next month looks to be a positive catalyst for shares and a stable growth guide out of the company for fiscal 2027 should help to reignite multiple expansion in this name. Trading at a compelling valuation with a robust free cash flow profile, Deckers is a buy.

Business Profile

Deckers is the owner of both the HOKA brand and the UGG brand. These two multi-billion dollar brands have gone through their own fashion cycles at different times and that has been what drives the business. UGG has maintained consistent popularity post-COVID, while HOKA has essentially emerged as a consequence of COVID, with the brand seeing impressive growth over the last 6 years. The company also operates other small brands like Teva, but it has been cleaning up this smaller portion of the portfolio. For example, the company sold the Koolaburra brand in fiscal Q3 2025 and sold the Sanuk brand during fiscal Q2 2025.

In terms of mix, HOKA accounts for 45% of sales, UGG accounts for 51% of sales and Other is the balance. In terms of geographic mix, the company is primarily domestic at 64% of sales, while international is 36% of sales. Deckers is light on disclosure, but similar to other domestic footwear companies, EMEA is larger for Deckers than APAC is. Then, in terms of channel mix, the company is actually majority wholesale at 57% and 43% direct to consumer (DTC).

The company sells its footwear products into sporting goods retailers as well as department stores and its own retail stores. It's widely available in the US now and has essentially completed its wholesale door expansion that it started in the early aftermath of COVID.

Financial Profile & Forward Estimates

Deckers on paper seems like a stable business, albeit one subject to consumer spending. This hardly tells the story. Towards the end of 2024, Deckers was approaching nearly $250/share ahead of earnings. It was one of the best performers in all of consumer retail. The driving force behind the stock's re-rating from 2022-2024 was principally HOKA growth. The company bought HOKA years ago for essentially nothing and scaled it in the post-COVID era seemingly overnight. Growth rates were compounding in the 20-30% region and investors were pleased.

Calendar Q1 2025 earnings were when the investors' worst fears started to compound. HOKA growth started to slow. What was a high-momentum, high-flying stock was now just a normalizing footwear company with forthcoming dependence on the consumer spending environment, rather than a rapidly growing brand in charge of its own destiny.

Enough time has passed now where investors should be able to give Deckers a fresh look, especially as HOKA is starting to return to growth.

It's important to note that Deckers doesn't provide quarterly guidance. Each earnings release will have only annual guidance. Segment guidance is also hard to come by for channel performance and profitability of the brands.

Recall the guidance:

  • Net sales $5.4-$5.425 billion
    • HOKA +mid-teens % y/y
    • UGG +mid-single-digits % y/y
  • Gross margin 57%
  • SG&A expenses 34.5%
  • Operating margin 22.5%
  • Tax rate 23%
  • Diluted EPS $6.80-$6.85

There is only one quarter left in the company's fiscal year, which is different timing than most standard calendar reporters. This makes the next earnings report all the more important as they'll be giving a new full year guide for FY27.

HOKA has a particularly easy compare as sales grew just 10% (off of their 20-30% pacing in quarters prior to that) and UGG is lapping only 3.6% growth. This past quarter, for context, HOKA was lapping 23.7% growth. So, the compare is nearly 14 points easier q/q. If the company is able to maintain the momentum it saw this past quarter at +18.5%, then we'd be looking at a near 30%+ growth rate for 4Q, which would really catch the market by surprise. That's not needed for the stock to work, but it is a possibility.

For Q4, I'm looking for revenue of $1.18 billion. This assumes 6% UGG growth, 15% HOKA growth and flat Other revenue. This is relative to street at $1.08 billion. This is a material top line beat.

Gross margins should be down y/y about 50 basis points, similar to last quarter, driven by tariffs. SG&A will be a lever to help offset that gross margin decline and I expect leverage, especially as HOKA performs better. I'm looking for 60bps of SG&A leverage. I shake out to about $203 million in operating profit. After factoring in a small gain from other income and a normal tax rate of 21%, we get net income of $173 million and Diluted EPS of $1.21. This compares to the street estimate of $0.83. This should be a clean report that sets up a great FY27 guide.

In thinking about how they could guide for the year ahead, recognize that Deckers aims to be conservative in their initial guide so they can have upside to estimates throughout the year. I think it's worth noting the compares for the brands as they're real drivers of growth in the first half of next fiscal year.

The company will likely not go for immediate double-digit growth in guidance, but a mid to high single digit growth guide is not out of the question. I would expect gross margins to stabilize as now tariffs are in the base, so there should be relatively small movements there. In accordance with some leverage, grosses should be up 10-30 basis points.

Then, if we think about SG&A, the company continues to be lean on costs as things like tariffs have presented pressure on the gross margin line, so I would expect continued leverage, albeit less than 2025. I'm looking for 30bps of leverage. If we put that all together, we're looking at just shy of $6 billion in revenue for FY27. Flowing that through my margin assumptions and we arrive at nearly $7.50 in EPS. This is relative to street at $7.34. That may not seem like much, but it's worth remembering that Deckers has seen negative earnings estimate revisions since January of 2025. As estimates have come down, so has the stock. If DECK can start to not just beat earnings on upcoming reports but show upside to guidance versus street estimates, this would result in a positive development that could lead to multiple expansion.

Cheap Relative Valuation

It's appropriate to do a relative valuation here because there are many public footwear players. While they all have their own unique portfolio positions, there is a lot of overlap. The only one I didn't include on the US side was Crocs (CROX). Crocs tends to have more fashion risk with its product set and while it has similar DTC/wholesale channel mix, it is not in the athletic subsector of footwear. As such, the peers listed below are more appropriate. Furthermore, Crocs does not play in the specialty running space, which is material to the HOKA business.

  • DECK trades at 15.8x forward earnings and EV/EBITDA of 10.4x
  • On Running (ONON) trades at 25.4x forward earnings and EV/EBITDA of 21.3x
  • Nike (NKE) trades at 29.8x forward earnings and EV/EBITDA of 19.8x
  • Wolverine (WWW) trades at 12.3x forward earnings and EV/EBITDA of 10x

The argument I think you can make here with DECK is that if the company is able to sustain a high-single-digit top line growth rate, that it should be trading in the high-teens if not low-20s. I don't think that it warrants closing the gap versus that of ONON or NKE given growth/brand power, but the margin profile on DECK is also one of the primary reasons behind why this should re-rate. This is a company with 23% operating margins. NKE would be lucky to get 11%. ONON has mid-teens operating margins. WWW has high single digit operating margins.

I think a reasonable price target is 19x $7.50 next year = $142 stock, representing 32% upside from the current level of $108.

Source: Passage Research

That profitability gets put on display when we consider the company will likely finish the year with about $1.2-$1.3 billion in free cash flow. It has already produced over $1 billion YTD. That's a near 6.7% free cash flow yield for best in class margins, great growth at high-single digits, and a conservative management team leading the way against easy compares in HOKA and leveraging a stable UGG business.

Risks

Deckers is subject to numerous risks, the most principal being consumer spending. If consumer confidence is weak and the willingness to spend on new footwear is low, the company will be negatively impacted. Next, the risk of the "chunky sole" silhouette is a fashion risk the customer faces. The majority of the footwear that HOKA produces is in this variety and with the real lack of a lifestyle vertical, it may be more susceptible to changes in fashion trends.

Other risks include competition from specialty running peers like On Running (ONON) as well as macro factors like tariffs. The company largely produces its footwear in Vietnam and Southeast Asia, which creates ongoing tariff risk.

Conclusion

Deckers has seen its fair share of investor criticism over the past few years, but the tide looks to be turning for them. The stock is trading at an attractive valuation versus peers, especially when we consider the free cash flow and operating margin profile. While earnings are still several weeks out, towards the end of May, the market may start to notice this reasonably priced growth story in consumer discretionary.

data.title: TimesSquare U.S. Mid Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
In the first quarter, markets navigated a complex backdrop of geopolitical tensions and economic resilience, alongside temporary global tariffs following a Supreme Court decision. Prior to the Iran conflict, markets had reached all-time highs with lower volatility, a dynamic that reversed following U.S. and Israeli engagement. Oil prices spiked, supply chains were disrupted, financial conditions tightened, and investors shifted toward safer assets. The conflict in Iran further underscored the growing importance of national resilience, prompting a re-evaluation of global supply chains and energy dependencies and accelerating the shift away from efficiency toward resilience and strategic autonomy. Central banks largely held policy steady in response to energy-driven inflation, with both the Federal Reserve and the ECB adopting a wait-and-see approach. Meanwhile, Japan's snap election delivered political stability and a mandate for fiscal expansion, despite ongoing concerns around long-term debt sustainability.

Annualized
1Q26 1YR 3YR 5YR 7YR 10YR
TimesSquare U.S. Mid Cap Growth Composite (Gross) -7.53% 5.40% 10.07% 5.69% 11.21% 12.28%
TimesSquare U.S. Mid Cap Growth Composite (NET) -7.72% 4.56% 9.21% 4.86% 10.34% 11.40%
Russell Midcap® Growth Index -6.35% 9.56% 12.73% 5.37% 10.27% 11.68%
Please see the important performance and other related disclosures at the end of this Commentary, which are an integral part of this quarterly Commentary Newsletter.

Amidst first-quarter volatility, the portfolio underperformed the Russell Midcap® Growth Index.

For the Communication Services sector, we prefer to invest in media and services companies that are either well placed from an advertising perspective for their target audience or that provide differentiated services.

Take-Two Interactive Software Inc. develops, publishes, and markets interactive entertainment, including titles such as Grand Theft Auto, LA Noire, Max Payne, Midnight Club, and Red Dead Redemption. Its shares sold off -23% amid speculation that Google's launch of Project Genie will affect game-developing companies. In our research, we determined that Genie does not pose a threat and believe the most important aspect is each company's intellectual property. Take-Two's fiscal third-quarter results give us confidence, and management reiterated that Grand Theft Auto 6 will launch in November. We added to the position on weakness.

Pinterest Inc. is an image-based social media platform. We exited our position following slower-than-expected fourth-quarter results and cautious guidance, attributed to advertising pullbacks by retailers impacted by tariffs. Its shares were down -44% while held in the quarter. The combination of softer-than-expected results along with lackluster forward guidance led us to liquidate the position. New to the sector this quarter was

Live Entertainment Inc. , which operates as a live entertainment company worldwide. Its business comprises the Concerts, Ticketing, and Sponsorship & Advertising segments. Importantly, the company reached a settlement with the Department of Justice in March to resolve an antitrust lawsuit. By doing so, artists will have flexibility in choosing their promoters and ticketing strategy, with the goal of keeping concert costs more affordable for fans.

Our preferences in the Consumer-oriented sectors lean toward value-oriented or specialty retailers, franchise models, premium brands, or support services for other consumer companies.

Casey's General Stores Inc. , an operator of 2,900 convenience stores across nineteen states, gained 32%. Reported mixed fiscal third-quarter results that included a top-line miss, with beats to EBITDA and earnings. SSS grew by 4%, accelerating from the fiscal second quarter. Their outlook remains solid with strong gross margins supported by a favorable mix. Geopolitical volatility has historically been net positive for fuel spreads. The bigger driver is chicken wings, with the expansion of this offering to more than 550 stores.

BJ's Wholesale Club Holdings Inc. operates membership warehouse clubs in the eastern half of the U.S. Fourth quarter results outpaced the consensus on better same-store sales and higher gross margins from gasoline sales, leading to a 9% rise in the stock price.

Ross Stores Inc. saw its shares advance 21% over the quarter after reporting outstanding results. Execution across marketing, customer engagement, new brands, and the store experience demonstrates that their off-price/value-focused model is resonating.

DoorDash Inc. operates a commerce platform that connects merchants, consumers, and dashers. The combination of a mixed fourth quarter and lower-than-expected first-quarter guidance led to a -34% sell-off. Of note, there was a record number of new Dash Pass enrollees. With investments front-end loaded, DoorDash is positioned for a second-half margin expansion, and that is why we added to the position.

We often see the ebb and flow of the Energy sector tied to underlying commodity prices. In this area, we seek low-cost exploration & production companies with high-yielding acreage or specialized service providers.

Cheniere Energy Inc. operates liquefied natural gas terminals in New Orleans and Corpus Christi. Heightened geopolitical tensions with Iran and structural damage to Qatari LNG facilities have sidelined 20% of global capacity, significantly tightening the supply-demand balance. This along with solid fourth-quarter earnings and increased forward guidance drove a 47% surge in the stock price. The company maintained elevated share buybacks and increased its authorization for the 2026-2030 period.

Permian Resources (PR) is an independent oil and natural gas company with operations in the Delaware Basin. Escalating geopolitical tensions with Iran and the subsequent rally in oil prices have driven a significant appreciation in Permian Resources' stock. The combination of better price realizations and lower operating expenses contributed to a strong quarter. We decided to exit this small position on the 35% improvement in its stock price.

Targa Resources Corp. owns, operates, acquires, and develops natural gas infrastructure assets. The geopolitical tensions in Iran contributed to stock price appreciation. A solid fourth quarter, driven by strong production volumes, led to a 37% stock price increase. Management announced increased planned capital expenditures and announced construction of a new natural gas liquids (NGL) plant in Delaware, as well as expansion of their NGL pipeline.

New to the sector this quarter was Cameco Corp. , which supplies uranium for electricity generation in the Americas, Europe, and Asia. They own and operate some of the largest uranium mines and sell to utility companies across sixteen different countries.

In the Financials sector, we tend to avoid banks experiencing credit deterioration or rising deposit costs, preferring asset managers, specialized insurance companies, or financial technology providers.

Electronic broker Interactive Brokers Group Inc. edged forward by 4% over the quarter. Account growth and trading volume were contributing factors in their latest results. Alternative asset manager TPG Inc. retreated by -36%, along with other alternative asset managers, primarily due to a sharp, sector-wide rotation out of the sector, driven by investor fears over software exposure and AI disruption. While no limited partners have stepped back to date, management indicated that fundraising may slow and monetization could be delayed. Of note, TPG announced a long-term strategic partnership with Jackson Financial, a move that significantly bolsters its insurance-linked fee income and credit scale. We have scaled back the position size.

Robinhood Markets Inc. operates a financial services platform enabling users to invest in stocks, exchange-traded funds, American depository receipts, and cryptocurrency. In the latest results, core earnings were weaker due to lower net revenue, resulting in a -39% pullback. Management struck a constructive tone on the 2026 outlook and expects revenue growth to continue to exceed expense growth. We had trimmed back the position prior to Q4 earnings.

Our preferences among Health Care stocks are those companies providing novel therapies for unmet needs that command premium pricing, or specialized service providers.

Insmed Inc. , a developer of therapies for patients with serious and rare diseases, dropped -6%. The launch of Brinsupri is well ahead of expectations. That medication received FDA approval last summer as the first treatment for non-cystic fibrosis bronchiectasis, a chronic lung disease. Management also noted that Arikayce sales were below the Street. That prescription antibiotic is used to treat refractory Mycobacterium avium complex lung disease. In our view, Brinsupri is their primary driver, with expectations of $1 billion in sales for 2026. We added to the position.

Stevanato Group (STVN) supplies drug containment, drug delivery, and diagnostic solutions to the biopharmaceutical industry. Its shares declined by -32% on market concerns that the shift to oral GLP1 drug formulations could slow growth in injectable products. However, we believe these concerns may be overblown as Stevanato has several multi-year contracts representing strong revenue visibility.

Repligen Corp. is a global life sciences company offering bioprocessing technologies and solutions used in manufacturing biological drugs. Fourth quarter revenues and earnings were ahead of the consensus. Forward guidance factored in uncertainty regarding biopharmaceutical industry capital expenditures and the pace of FDA approvals causing the stock to sell off by -28%.

New to the sector this quarter was Ionis Pharmaceuticals Inc. , a commercial-stage biotechnology company focused on neurology and cardiovascular indications. They have several commercially approved drugs plus a deep pipeline with important clinical trial readouts this year.

Many of our Industrial positions provide necessary business-to-business operational services, highly technical components, equipment enabling automation & efficiency improvements, or essential infrastructure services.

EMCOR Group Inc. provides electrical, mechanical, construction, industrial, and facilities services. The stock price gained 21% on the heels of a strong fourth quarter. 2026 guidance surpassed Street estimates. The company continues to benefit from the booming demand for data center infrastructure. It is an essential "pick-and-shovel" play for the construction of massive data centers required by AI hyper-scalers.

Carpenter Technology Corp. manufactures, fabricates, and distributes specialty metals. Its share price jumped 27% after management confirmed a rebound in aerospace demand.

Rising 24% was Curtis Wright Corp. , a provider of engineered products, solutions, and services to aerospace, defense, commercial nuclear power, and industrial markets. Fourth quarter revenues beat sell-side expectations and that led to higher earnings. Their aerospace and defense business was a highlight, led by ground defense and naval defense.

Regal Rexnord Corp. , a provider of power and control motion products, climbed 34% rising on the back of impressive contract wins for its data center power management solutions. Mixed results included a slight beat on earnings and a slight miss on revenue. Orders were, however, a standout, leaping 54% y/y with wins in the company's new datacenter power management offering.

Verisk Analytics Inc. provides data, analytics, and technology solutions to the insurance industry. While there were some concerns heading into fourth-quarter earnings, the company delivered solid results, a large share buyback, in-line forward guidance, and the rollout of AI-enabled solutions for clients. Its stock price dropped by -15% in the quarter.

Among the wide variety of Information Technology companies, we prefer critical systems providers, specialized component designers, systems that improve client productivity or efficiency, and others that are growing their share of corporate IT budgets.

Lattice Semiconductor Corp. develops semiconductor, silicon-based, silicon-enabled products including programmable gate arrays. A solid quarter was rewarded with a 26% run-up in the stock price. Management highlighted that the demand signals strengthened over the last quarter, and first quarter guidance was well ahead of projections. Robust demand for data centers and AI-related physical infrastructure supported revenue upside.

Monolithic Power Systems Inc. provides semiconductor-based power electronics solutions to direct current as well as convert and control voltages for electronic systems. The company is benefiting from the broader enthusiasm for AI picks and shovels as investors position themselves for continued growth in data center spending. They reported a modest fourth-quarter beat and gave solid first-quarter guidance. The major surprise from the earnings call was that management raised full-year enterprise data segment growth, which boosted the stock by 21%.

Onestream Inc. delivers a unified, AI-enabled, and extensible software platform. The company entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by private equity firm Hg in an all-cash transaction. Its shares rose 29% while held in the quarter.

HubSpot Inc. offers a cloud-based customer relationship management platform. We exited the position due to the combination of potential AI disruption and the seat-based model's potential challenges. The stock tumbled -43% before it was sold.

ServiceTitan Inc. provides an end-to-end cloud-based software platform to connect and manage a range of business workflows for trade-based businesses. Its shares declined by -40% as investors sold everything software-related. The company has been gaining traction in new markets, supported by recent enterprise wins and increased adoption of its Pro products and AI virtual agents, with contract sizes doubling. Fiscal fourth quarter results were solid, with overall subscription revenues above the street. There was continued strength in Pro products, commercial, and roofing. Qualitative commentary from management suggests an accelerating growth potential as AI usage revenues ramp, and that is not baked into guidance.

JFrog Ltd. offers a platform for software supply chain management. It continues to experience significant cloud growth, driven by increased AI adoption and higher data-transfer intensity. Despite reporting a strong fourth-quarter 2025 beat, the stock fell -25% after being caught in the "Claude trade" sell-off. As has been common recently, investors reacted to AI-related fears first and assessed fundamental reality later.

New to the sector this quarter was MKS Inc. , a provider of foundational technology solutions to semiconductor manufacturing, electronics & packaging, and specialty industrial applications. The company is well positioned to benefit from strong semiconductor AI-related spending across foundry, logic, and memory.

Within the real estate sector, CBRE Group Inc. , a commercial real estate services and investment company, dropped by -16%. Results from the latest quarter were high quality across business lines. Nevertheless, commercial real estate brokers have come under pressure due to concerns about potential AI disintermediation, which we believe is low.

CoStar Group Inc. provides information, analytics, and marketplace services to the real estate industry. Reported solid fourth quarter results and reiterated guidance for 2026, although net new bookings were a touch light of buy-side expectations.

Trends in the core business remain strong, and profitability continues to outpace guidance and estimates. Its shares tumbled -40% in the quarter.

We are moving into a world where capital is increasingly directed by strategic necessity rather than pure economic returns, transforming energy, defense, and supply chains into critical instruments of national policy. Our investment team are closely monitoring interest rate paths, geopolitical developments in Iran, and the evolving regulatory landscape following recent trade-related legal rulings. While these macro forces and shifting trade frameworks create headlines and near-term volatility, they also generate the dislocations where our fundamental, bottom-up approach thrives. We remain focused on identifying disciplined management teams capable of compounding value by navigating this new era of complexity. As always, we are available to answer any questions you may have.

data.title: American Express: Earnings Show Steady Growth, Maintain Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Its the most wonderful time of the year, for market lovers at least. I'm talking about earnings season of course. That chance to get a quarterly read on our favorite stocks, a chance to evaluate if our investment theses remain robust. Last week we got the update from American Express (NYSE:AXP) and I am please to say my buy thesis remains very much in place.

Since my last coverage of the stock, the price has been quite volatile. In my prior coverage the stock was buy rated at a price level of $316, this was at the start of 2025 with a relatively constructive outlook. Sentiment drove the stock volatility rather than fundamentals. The liberation day tariff announcement led to a sharp sell off, which was quickly followed by a very strong rally as the initial tariff announcements were pared back as trade deals were signed with key partners.

Moving into 2026, the stock has been under pressure as the war in the Middle East and the associated increase in fuels costs has the market worried about the resilience of consumers to keep spending. AXP after all runs a very spend-centric business model, so consumer confidence is a key driver of growth. I would encourage readers to focus on the fundamentals of the business rather than market sentiment. We got numbers last week from AXP and to my eyes it is very much a nothing to see here result. AXP is humming along at a nice clip, notching up sustainable steady growth. This is a very high quality company with a premium customer base, maintain buy on a strong set of earnings.

Earnings

You would be hard pressed to pick many holes in AXP's Q1 earnings number last week. Revenue grew by 11% in the quarter and EPS grew by 18%. Billed business, a measure of volume and indicative of overall market trends, saw an acceleration to 9% growth in the quarter. Billed business has now seen accelerating growth each quarter since Q1 2025, these business fundamentals paint a clear picture of a strong and improving business as opposed to market fears of a weakening consumer and a decline in spending.

Billed business on the commercial side, where AXP provides business cards to SMBs and corporates, showed a slight improvement but admittedly is on the softer side with just 4% growth. International business was a solid 13% growth when adjusted for FX movements, looking at it purely on a reported basis it showed a strong acceleration to 20% growth. All this paints a picture of US consumers are strong and spending well, US businesses are a bit softer but remain resilient and AXP continues to resonate well internationally as it expands its business overseas.

So that's one side of the earnings story for AXP, spending levels and growth. The other side, equally as important, is from a credit perspective how is the consumer managing. The answer to that question is the consumer is doing just fine. Net write-offs, a measure of loans to customers which will not be repaid, actually improved to 2% from 2.1% the prior year. This clearly points to a healthy and resilient consumer that continues to spend money and is perfectly capable of meeting interest repayments.

What explains the resilience? I suspect this is the K-shaped economy at work. There is undoubtedly some level of consumer stress in the economy, inflation has been above trend for several years, food and energy prices are high, so households which spend a higher percentage of their budget on those items will fell the pinch. American Express by nature targets a more premium customer base, they screen for high FICO scores and their customer cohort will be folks with gainful employment and healthy domestic balance sheets. These customers are less affected by inflation in the economy and they retain the ability to spend on discretionary items like eating out and traveling.

Finally, AXP maintained its full year guidance for revenue growth close to 10% and EPS growth in the teens. The negative share price reaction following the earnings release I suspect may have been driven by the unchanged guidance. The market is asking the question, if AXP delivered north of 10% revenue growth and high-teens EPS growth in Q1, why not increase rather than maintain guidance?

Its a fair question to ask, but I think the market is reading too much into it. We are one quarter into the year and the situation in the Middle East remains uncertain. AXP has delivered a strong quarter, it is no harm taking the prudent approach and leaving guidance unchanged for now, it is early days in 2026. I fully expect AXP will be beating this guide when the year is all said and done. The market is over-analyzing the implications of unchanged guidance in my mind.

Valuation

The valuation of AXP is pretty much average compared to historical norms. The average AXP valuation looks even more compelling when we consider the broader market is trading at relatively elevated levels. Given we have just seen a very tidy set of Q1 numbers which displayed both resilient credit metrics and an acceleration in billings growth, AXP looks an attractive buy at these levels.

Furthermore, the chart of AXP's stock price overlaid with the forward EPS estimate shows a breakdown from trend. This indicates to me that the earnings continue to march higher, while the price has fallen back on sentiment driven trading. I like to stick to the fundamentals, everything from Q1 results and analyst's forecasts suggests to me that AXP is well placed.

Risks

The obvious risk to AXP, and the economy in general, is a significantly prolonged conflict in the Middle East which exposed bottlenecks in the global supply chain for things like energy fertilizer and specialty products like Helium. Oil at $100 is not exactly ideal but I suspect it is not economy breaking, Oil at $100 today is not that same as it was in the 1970s or 1990s. We have seen significant inflation in other segments of the economy and there is simply more money sloshing around the system in general, wages have increased substantially over the decades too. So while $100 oil is inconvenient and will pinch some consumers for sure, I suspect the premium AXP crowd will manage fine.

However, if a prolonged war leads oil to rise to say $150, then we might have a problem. Oil at those levels could cause genuine demand destruction, energy-intensive industries could have to halt or shut down business, layoffs could spike. The premium AXP customer would again be better able to cope with such economic difficulties but I think it reasonable to thing they might pare back on the fancy restaurants and hotel stays for a bit.

Conclusion

Q1 earnings paint a picture of a well run company that continues to execute on its strategy. The stock of AXP is far more volatile than the underlying business. This is a company that has steadily notched double-digit earnings growth several years in a row and it is well on course to do the same again this year. Shares are trading at a fairly average level vs history, at a time when the broader market trades at a meaningful premium to history.

American Express is a great brand, a great company and a great stock to own. While volatile at times, I think this is an excellent stock that has rightful place in most portfolios. Think of that stock as a play on American growth and that growth of affluence more broadly. In a market moment where many industries are facing the scrutiny of how they might be disrupted by AI, I see leading brands as well insulated as customer loyalty is driven as much by brand desirability rather than technical factors. American Express has been a leading American company for decades and I expect it will continue to be for many decades more.

data.title: Equity Outlook Q2 2026: Global Growth Holds Firm As Geopolitical Risk Simmers
data.description:
By Amr Hanafy

Persistent Macro Tailwinds and Broadening Earnings Growth Keep Us Constructive on Risk Assets Through the Fog of War

“We remain encouraged by an ongoing global industrial rebound, broadening of capex beyond tech, and improving earnings growth. Our base case implies the U.S./Iran conflict de-escalates within the next few weeks.

Yet we are by no means complacent: Potential protracted conflict in Iran, softening cyclical employment and fading liquidity momentum represent meaningful near-term risks to global equity markets.”

Part 1: The Macro Story Remains Intact

  • Global industrial activity continues to expand
  • Global credit remains above pre-pandemic trend
  • U.S. labor conditions remain healthy
  • Larger U.S. tax refunds may support discretionary spending

Part 2: Markets Continue to Broaden

  • Capex growth is broadening beyond tech
  • EPS growth for the median stock continues to improve
  • Rotation toward value stocks and small caps may resume as growth continues to rebound
  • Institutional equity positioning could rise strongly as volatility subsides

Part 3: Risks to Our Thesis

  • Higher energy prices could have a non-linear negative impact on global growth and inflation
  • An oil shock could erode consumers’ real purchasing power just as their savings buffers have thinned
  • A meaningful market pullback could trigger a reverse wealth effect

Part 4: Portfolio Considerations

  • Regions: Remain overweight Japan and EM; upgrade U.S. to overweight; downgrade Europe to underweight
  • U.S. sectors: Maintain overweight Energy and Technology (a cyclical/growth barbell)
  • U.S. sizes and styles: Equal weight large caps vs. small caps, and value vs. growth

Part 1: The Macro Story Remains Intact

Despite ongoing geopolitical conflicts and growing concern about near-term market volatility, we believe the fundamental case for global economic growth remains intact and broadly supportive of risk assets. After a 3-year global manufacturing slump, a rising number of countries have been reporting 50+ PMIs (top chart) and global credit continues to run above its pre-pandemic trend (bottom chart).

Multiple indicators suggest to us that the industrial rebound is broadening beyond the U.S. technology sector. We believe new orders less inventories is pointing toward a headline PMI index in the mid-to-high 50s (top chart) and that the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA) could drive meaningful capex growth, especially in the first half of 2026 (bottom chart).

Growth in average weekly payrolls (a proxy for nominal income and spending) remains resilient and close to its average pre-pandemic level (top chart). We believe payroll growth will likely support U.S. consumption, which accounts for roughly 70% of U.S. GDP. While the Labor Market Conditions Index (a comprehensive indicator) has softened, overall labor conditions remain healthy (bottom left) and increased tax refunds due to the OBBBA could further support near-term discretionary spending (bottom right).

Part 2: Markets Continue to Broaden

While U.S. hyper-scalers continue to drive overall capex investment, the rest of the economy has begun to follow suit. Capex growth within the rest of the S&P 500 has ticked up and is beginning to broaden out (top chart). Analysts are now revising capex estimates higher (bottom chart).

We believe improving corporate fundamentals will continue to drive the broadening-out trade: The median stock across the Russell 3000 Index (which includes small-cap, mid-cap and large-cap names) has seen steady improvement in EPS growth over the last eight quarters.

Thus far in 2026, we have seen a consistent pattern across the entire investment style box, with value stocks and small caps outperforming growth stocks and large caps. After a pause during the Iran conflict, we believe these recent dynamics could resume and have the potential to endure over the near to mid term.

Rising oil prices have rattled energy-dependent equity markets, such as Japan, Korea, and the eurozone, and threaten to thwart the broader global industrial recovery (top chart). However, history suggests to us that sell-offs driven by geopolitical events can be relatively short-lived and be followed by sharp rebounds(bottom chart). Assuming the conflict subsides within the next few weeks, we believe markets with relatively solid fundamentals could rerate on rebounding investor sentiment.

Institutional investors have reduced exposure to risk assets in the wake of the Iran conflict. Equity positioning has fallen from the 70th percentile level to the 27th percentile level (top chart); meanwhile, allocations to safer, cash-equivalent vehicles have sharply increased (bottom chart). We believe this setup offers more room for upside to risk assets once the fog of war begins to clear.

Part 3: Key Risks to Our Thesis

History suggests to us that sharp and swift increases in the price of oil—a key input in many energy and industrial markets—tend to coincide with recessions (top chart). Our base case assumes that the U.S./Iran war de-escalates within the next few weeks.

Yet if the recently reopened Strait of Hormuz once again becomes a point of contention, we fear further interruption in oil flows and supply chains could have non-linear negative impacts on global growth and inflation and negatively impact risk assets (bottom chart).

We believe that elevated energy prices could further pressure consumers’ discretionary spending when it is already in decline (top chart); meanwhile, overall consumer spending has been outpacing income growth for over a year, squeezing real purchasing power just as savings buffers have thinned.

And with households sitting at record equity allocations, we worry a meaningful market pullback could trigger a reverse wealth effect and potentially put additional pressure on discretionary spending (bottom chart).

Growth in cyclical employment—the more economically sensitive segment of the labor market—continues to decline, albeit at a slower pace (top chart). We believe broader deployment of AI could apply more pressure, though company announcements suggest that overall market conditions have been driving far more job cuts than deployment of AI (bottom chart). In short, AI’s impact on the labor market is visible, but still relatively muted.

Part 4: Portfolio Considerations

Regions: Remain overweight Japan and EM

We remain constructive on global equities, with overweights in both Japan and EM.

Both markets are inherently more cyclical and therefore more geared to global industrial production (top left chart).

Following a 3-year manufacturing slump, we believe these markets could outperform as global growth recovers.

We expect the USD to continue weakening as global growth rebounds, an environment that potentially bodes well for EM stocks outperforming those in developed markets (top right).

Finally, the share of global equity flows into ex-U.S. markets continues to increase (bottom).

Regions: Upgrade U.S. to Overweight

Overall, we believe the U.S. macro backdrop remains solid and able to weather the current energy shock better than other major economies. We expect fiscal and monetary policy, along with accelerating private-sector capex (increasingly driven by AI-related investment), will continue to support growth. Furthermore, the U.S. economy’s oil intensity has dropped significantly over the past 50 years: Just 0.3 barrels are consumed per $1,000 of GDP today, compared to 0.9 barrels during the 1973 oil embargo.

Meanwhile, liquidity has provided a timely cushion during the U.S./Iran conflict and continues to support financial conditions (top chart). And with valuations meaningfully de-rated, earnings and sales revisions still positive (bottom chart), and institutional positioning still relatively modest, we believe U.S. equities warrant a slight upgrade.

Regions: Downgrade Europe to Underweight

We believe the risk-reward outlook in European equities has turned unfavorable. Gas reserves in the Eurozone are well below average levels, amplifying the region’s exposure to a protracted energy-supply shock (top left chart) and potential inflation, prompting the ECB to tighten policy into economic weakness rather than strength. Meanwhile, real money supply growth—a leading indicator of economic activity—could already be peaking (top right).

Earnings growth in Europe is mainly a cyclical story: EPS growth for cyclical sectors in 2026 is expected to be 31% vs. just 5% for defensive sectors, with autos and banks (two key cyclical sectors) accounting for roughly half of total EPS growth (bottom chart). We fear a slowdown in manufacturing activity or ill-timed rate hikes could put the bulk of that cyclical equities growth at risk.

Size: Equal weight U.S. large caps vs. U.S. small caps

While we remain constructive on U.S. small caps, we believe the macro backdrop now favors high-quality U.S. large caps, especially amidst rising geopolitical risk and uncertainty.

The forward P/E on the S&P 500 Index has fallen 10% from its October 2025 peak on fears of AI disruption and the impacts of the conflict in Iran. Yet since the conflict began, forward earnings estimates among large caps have risen by 5%, with technology firms accounting for two-thirds of that increase (top chart).

Within the broader large-cap group, the Mag 7 now trade at 26x forward earnings. Relative to the S&P 493, that’s a level near the troughs of the late-2022 sell-off and Liberation Day (bottom chart), all while earnings growth for the group has remained resilient. From a risk-reward standpoint, we believe this setup could provide an attractive entry point.

Sectors: Energy

We believe the energy sector continues to offer a compelling combination of strong fundamentals, investment flows and relative valuations.

Relative performance of energy stocks tends to track the price of oil, which has spiked during the Iran conflict (top left chart). We believe the sector’s earnings estimates do not yet reflect potentially higher-for-longer oil prices (top right).

After years of outflows, investor sentiment is now beginning to turn, yet we believe positioning is still far from stretched (bottom left). And even after the recent rally, we think relative valuations remain attractive (bottom right).
